clubs.name: The Page Turners
clubs.description: A welcoming club for readers who love deep conversation about character, theme, and the sentences that stay with you.

meetings.notes: Bring your favorite passage. We'll start with the green light symbol.
Berkeley AHC curated list kickoff. Read Ch. 1–3; bring one ad and one painting to compare.

threads.prompt: Is the American Dream aspirational or destructive here?
threads.author: Alex

replies.author: Jordan
replies.text: I think Fitzgerald makes the Dream aspirational only in retrospect — Gatsby's hope is magnificent, but the machinery that funds it is rotten. The dream survives in Nick's narration, not in Gatsby's outcome.

replies.author: Sam
replies.text: The green light works because it's always across the water — close enough to orient your life around, never close enough to possess. That's the Dream's cruel geometry.

threads.prompt: 'Freeing yourself was one thing; claiming ownership of that freed self was another.'
threads.author: Riley

replies.author: Alex
replies.text: Sethe's arc is about the second clause — legal freedom didn't grant her ownership of her story or her motherhood. Beloved forces that reckoning.
